title: Polymer clay millefiori cane
subtitle: A patterned clay log that produces repeated slices for jewellery and decoration
excerpt: A millefiori cane is a log of polymer clay with a pattern running through its length, so every cross-section slice shows the same image. The simplest version is a bullseye: one colour wrapped around another. The cane is built before baking, reduced to the target diameter by rolling, then sliced and applied to beads, pendants, or tiles before the final bake.
sourceNotes: Millefiori cane technique. Derived from Venetian glass millefiori practice adapted for polymer clay. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pattern technique in which coloured clay layers are assembled into a log (cane) with a design running through its length. Slicing the cane reveals the same pattern in every slice.
A PVC-based modelling clay that hardens in a home oven at 130°C. Colours do not bleed into each other during building, making it ideal for cane work.
Rolling and stretching a cane to a smaller diameter without distorting the internal pattern.

content.text: means 'a thousand flowers' in Italian, but the technique starts with much simpler patterns. A bullseye cane, one colour wrapped around another, is the building block from which flowers, checkerboards, and stripes are all assembled. The key property of
that makes cane work possible is that the colours do not bleed into each other during building, they remain distinct until the cane is baked.

attrs.title: Work cool
attrs.body: Polymer clay softens from the heat of hands. Warm clay smears colour boundaries and makes cane patterns blurry. Work in a cool room and pause if the clay becomes sticky. Chilling the cane in the fridge for 10 minutes between reducing steps helps maintain crisp lines.

items.name: Pottery rolling pin or acrylic rod

content.text: Condition each colour separately until smooth. Keep them separate, do not let them touch.
Roll the first colour into a smooth log 20 cm long and 1.5 cm in diameter. Keep it even by rolling with both hands from the centre outward. The log becomes the core of the bullseye.

Roll the second colour into a flat sheet 2 mm thick using the rolling pin. Cut a rectangle the same length as the core log. Roll the sheet around the core, overlapping the edge slightly where the sheet meets itself. Blend the seam by rolling the cane on a flat surface until the join disappears and the outer layer is even thickness all around.

the cane to the diameter you want for slicing: roll it gently on a flat surface, working from the centre outward, until it is 8 to 10 mm in diameter for small beads, or 15 mm for larger pendants. Reducing slowly prevents air pockets forming inside the cane. Chill in the fridge for 10 minutes before slicing.

Slice the cane into 2 mm discs with the craft knife using a single-stroke press straight down, not a sawing motion. Discard the first few slices from each end which may be distorted. Apply the slices to pre-shaped raw beads by pressing firmly and rolling smooth. Bake at 130°C for 30 minutes.
Stack four bullseye canes together and wrap the bundle in another sheet for a flower pattern.
Apply slices to flat tile shapes of raw clay and smooth the seams flat before baking for a mosaic tile effect.

items.symptom: Pattern blurs at the colour boundary when reduced
items.cause: Clay too warm, or reduced too quickly.
items.fix: Chill the cane for 10 minutes and reduce again slowly, working from the centre outward. Colour boundaries are sharpest when the clay is cool and firm.

items.symptom: Cane has air bubbles visible in slices
items.cause: Core was not rolled evenly, leaving gaps, or the outer layer was not blended at the seam.
items.fix: Seal the seam carefully and roll the cane along the full length. A small bubble can be pressed out with a fingertip while the clay is still raw.
